1: Letters of recommendation (systatikaí epistolai, 2 Corinthians 3:1) were a standard social institution in the ancient Mediterranean world: a prominent person would write a letter attesting to the character, status, and worthiness of a traveling associate. Cicero wrote dozens (Ad Atticum; Ad Familiares); papyri preserve examples from Egyptian commerce. Paul's opponents brought such letters to Corinth, establishing their credentials through social networks Paul lacked or scorned. Paul's counterargument: the Corinthians themselves are his letter of recommendation — written on hearts by the Spirit, readable by everyone. The apostolic community is the authenticating credential for genuine ministry.
7: The contrast between the 'ministry of death, carved in letters on stone' (diakonia tou thanatou en grammasin entetypōmenē lithois) and the 'ministry of the Spirit' reflects Paul's understanding of Sinai. The Mosaic covenant was glorious — Moses's face shone (Exodus 34:29-35); the Israelites could not look at it. But that glory was transient (katargoumene, passing away, 2 Corinthians 3:7). Paul's argument uses a qal vahomer (lesser to greater) rabbinic form: if the lesser (Mosaic) had glory, the greater (Spirit-ministry) surpasses it immeasurably. This is not anti-Torah polemic (Paul quotes Torah approvingly throughout 2 Corinthians) but a salvation-historical ordering.
2: You yourselves are our letter of recommendation — in the Hellenistic world, letters of recommendation (epistolai systatikal) were essential for mobile teachers, traders, and officials who needed to establish credentials in new communities. 3 John 9-10 and Acts 18:27 both reference the practice. Paul inverts the convention: the community is the letter.
3: And you show that you are a letter from Christ delivered by us, written not with ink but with the Spirit of the living God, not on tablets of stone but on tablets of human hearts — the contrast between stone tablets and human hearts echoes Jeremiah 31:33 (the new covenant written on the heart) and Ezekiel 36:26 (a heart of flesh instead of a heart of stone). The inscribed-heart is the eschatological mark of the new covenant.
4: Such is the confidence (pepoithēsis, persuasion, settled conviction) that we have through Christ toward God — Paul's self-confidence is mediated through Christ (dia Christou pros ton theon). This preposition chain maps the apostolic mediation: confidence flows to God through the Son.
5: Not that we are sufficient (hikanoi, adequate, qualified) in ourselves to claim anything as coming from us, but our sufficiency is from God — this is the answer to the rhetorical question of 2 Corinthians 2:16. Hikanotēs (sufficiency, competence) was a term for the professional qualification of a tradesman or official.
6: Who has made us sufficient to be ministers (diakonous) of a new covenant — diakonos (servant, minister) appears throughout 2 Corinthians as Paul's preferred self-designation (2 Corinthians 3:6; 2 Corinthians 4:1; 2 Corinthians 6:4; 2 Corinthians 11:15, 23). The new covenant (kainē diathēkē) explicitly echoes Jeremiah 31:31-34 and the Last Supper tradition (1 Corinthians 11:25). Not of the letter (gramma) but of the Spirit.
8: Will not the ministry of the Spirit have even more glory? — the qal v'homer (how much more) argument was a standard rabbinic hermeneutical move: if the lesser is true, how much more the greater. Paul applies it to the comparison of the Mosaic and new covenant ministries.
9: For if there was glory in the ministry of condemnation (katakriseos) — the Mosaic ministry (the law that exposes and condemns sin) had real glory; Paul does not dismiss the Torah. But the ministry of righteousness (dikaiosynes) must far exceed it in glory — the comparative structure honors the old while asserting the new's surpassing quality.
10: Indeed, in this case, what once had glory has come to have no glory at all, because of the glory that surpasses it — the eclipse metaphor: a candle has real light, but in direct sunlight its light becomes invisible by comparison. The Torah's glory is real but relative.
11: For if what was being brought to an end came with glory, much more will what is permanent (menon) have glory — the contrast between the fading (katargoumenon) and the permanent (menon) echoes throughout the letter. The temporary/permanent distinction reflects apocalyptic thought about the two ages.
12: Since we have such a hope, we are very bold (parrēsia, boldness, frank speech) — parrēsia was the Athenian democratic ideal of free speech, the right of every citizen to speak openly before the assembly. Paul adopts the term for the apostolic freedom of speaking the gospel without the concealment that the old covenant ministry required.
13: Not like Moses, who would put a veil over his face so that the Israelites might not gaze at the outcome of what was being brought to an end — the Exodus 34:29-35 passage describes Moses covering his face with a masveh (veil) after encounters with God. Paul's reading adds the element of concealment of the fading nature of the Mosaic glory.
14: But their minds were hardened (epōrōthē, were made hard/callous) — the hardening language echoes Isaiah 6:9-10 (make the heart of this people dull) and its NT applications (Matthew 13:14-15; John 12:40; Acts 28:26-27). For to this day, when they read the old covenant, that same veil remains unlifted.
15: Yes, to this day whenever Moses is read a veil lies over their hearts — the synagogue reading of Moses (the Torah) was a regular sabbath practice. Paul describes the veil as lying over the heart (kardian) rather than the face — the location of understanding and will.
16: But when one turns to the Lord, the veil is removed — the turning (epistrephō) toward the Lord removes the veil; the same verb is used for conversion in the NT (Acts 3:19; 2 Corinthians 9:35; 2 Corinthians 26:18). The Exodus text says Moses removed the veil when he entered the presence of the Lord (Exodus 34:34).
17: Now the Lord is the Spirit, and where the Spirit of the Lord is, there is freedom (eleutheria) — the identification of the Lord (kyrios) with the Spirit generates one of the most discussed christological statements in Paul. The freedom (eleutheria) contrasts with the veil of concealment and the bondage of the old covenant.
18: And we all, with unveiled face, beholding the glory of the Lord, are being transformed (metamorphoumetha) into the same image from one degree of glory to another — the transformation by beholding reflects Hellenistic ideas about assimilation to what one contemplates (Plato, Republic 490a-b: the philosopher becomes like the divine through contemplation). Paul applies this to the Christian's ongoing transformation through the Spirit.
